tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.workaround:
Use vector aligned instructions:
  1. align your arrays on 64 bytes boundaries: replace { void *p = malloc (size); } with { void *p; posix_memalign (&p, 64, size); }.
  2. inform your compiler that your arrays are vector aligned: if array 'foo' is 64 bytes-aligned, define a pointer 'p_foo' as __builtin_assume_aligned (foo, 64) and use it instead of 'foo' in the loop.
<>

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 17.67 cycles. At this rate:
  • 17% of peak load performance is reached (16.98 out of 96.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 18% of peak store performance is reached (11.55 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks
expert.txt:
Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 17.67 to 13.00 cycles (1.36x speedup).

header: 6% of peak computational performance is used (1.58 out of 24.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
• Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
gain.title: Code clean check
gain.txt:
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 17.67 to 8.33 cycles (2.12x speedup).

gain.workaround: Read the "512-bits vectorization" report at "Potential" confidence level.
gain.details:
40% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):
  • 57% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 40% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 66% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
  • 0% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your loop is poorly vectorized.
Only 21% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).

gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt: Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

potential.title: 512-bits vectorization
potential.txt: On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

hint.workaround:
Use vector aligned instructions:
  1. align your arrays on 64 bytes boundaries: replace { void *p = malloc (size); } with { void *p; posix_memalign (&p, 64, size); }.
  2. inform your compiler that your arrays are vector aligned: if array 'foo' is 64 bytes-aligned, define a pointer 'p_foo' as __builtin_assume_aligned (foo, 64) and use it instead of 'foo' in the loop.
<>

header: 6% of peak computational performance is used (1.58 out of 24.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
• Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
gain.title: Code clean check
gain.txt:
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 17.67 to 8.33 cycles (2.12x speedup).

gain.workaround: Read the "512-bits vectorization" report at "Potential" confidence level.
gain.details:
40% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):
  • 57% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 40% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 66% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
  • 0% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your loop is poorly vectorized.
Only 21% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).

gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt: Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

potential.title: 512-bits vectorization
potential.txt: On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.
